sub_topics.detail: Cookie Advantage, Inc. is an Oklahoma corporation engaged in franchising Cookie Advantage businesses in various states. Franchisees sell various types of packaged cookie products to business enterprises. The Company earns a franchise fee of $34,900 upon completion of franchise agreement requirements and a royalty on ongoing sales. Two franchise territories were reacquired in 2024, and seventeen franchise locations were in operations at end of 2024.
Highly liquid and unrestricted demand deposit accounts with maturity of less than ninety days. Consists of checking accounts.

Short-term, non-interest bearing and uncollateralized. Doubtful accounts charged to operations in period deemed uncollectible based on status of payments. Receivables older than 30 days considered past due. Company considers receivables as of December 31, 2024, 2023, and 2022 to be fully collectible. No allowance for doubtful accounts is recorded.
Reacquired franchise rights are stated at cost and amortized over its useful life (15 years).

Operating leases included in operating lease right-of-use (ROU) assets, other current liabilities, and operating lease liabilities in the Company's balance sheet. Finance leases included in property and equipment, other current liabilities, and other long-term liabilities in the balance sheet. The Company had no material leases qualifying as ROU assets and liabilities as of December 31, 2024.

The Company considers an executed franchise disclosure document to be a contract with a customer. Revenues consist of franchise fees, renewal and transfer fees, technology fees and royalties. Revenues derived from sale of a franchise are recognized over the term of the franchise agreement (generally five years). The Company has multiple performance obligations to fulfill — the most substantial is to provide training to new franchisees prior to their first sale. Approximately 70% of all performance obligations are fulfilled within the first year. The remaining 30% related to the franchise fee are fulfilled in years two through five. Pre-opening services recognized as a single performance obligation. Royalty revenues based on percent of sales, recognized at time the underlying sales occur. The Company allows franchisees to renew their agreement after the initial five-year period is completed — franchisee's must be in good standing and in compliance with the initial Franchise Agreement. Renewal fee policy: recognize renewal fees when received upon start of new franchise term — no performance obligations to fulfill, amount is not refundable. Transfer fees: 100% recognized at time of transfer for training and related benefits provided to the new owner.

Revenues recognized when control of promised products is transferred to customers. Revenue recognized upon transfer of promised benefit in amount reflecting expected consideration. Five-step process: (1) Identify contract(s) with customer, (2) Identify performance obligations, (3) Determine transaction price, (4) Allocate transaction price, (5) Recognize revenue when performance obligations are met.

Factors that could impact nature, amount, timing, and uncertainty of revenue and cash flows: (1) vulnerability to cyclical nature of franchising industry; (2) demand for Company's services dependent upon demand for packaged cookie products; (3) timing of franchise sales outside Company's control.

S-corporation election under Internal Revenue Code. Stockholders pay taxes on Company's taxable income. No provision or liability for federal or state income taxes in financial statements. Management determined no uncertain tax positions or unrecognized benefits that would materially impact financial statements. Tax returns subject to examination for three years for federal and state.

Preparation of financial statements requires management estimates and assumptions affecting reported amounts. Actual results could differ from those estimates. Appropriate adjustments are made on a prospective basis. Reasonably possible that changes may occur in near term that would affect management's estimates including: fair value measurements, allowance for doubtful accounts, accrued expenses, and revenue recognition. Revisions to estimates made in year in which circumstances requiring revision become known.

In June 2016, FASB issued ASU 2016-13 Financial Instruments — Credit Losses (Topic 326). Requires a delay in recognizing credit losses until it is probable a loss has been incurred. Replaces incurred loss impairment methodology in current GAAP with methodology reflecting expected credit losses. Company adopted ASU 2016-13 as of January 1, 2023 under modified prospective approach. Adoption did not materially impact the financial statements.
Company has evaluated subsequent events through March 12, 2025, the date the financial statements were available to be issued.

notes.title: Related Party Transactions
notes.detail: Office and production facilities are provided by KDKC, Inc., an Oklahoma corporation affiliated by common ownership and management. All business and day-to-day operations are performed by the owners or employees of KDKC, Inc. For the years ended December 31, 2024, 2023 and 2022, management fees of $488,555, $459,151 and $420,000 respectively, were paid to KDKC, which owns and operates a Cookie Advantage business similar to the franchise operations described in Note 1. The amount owed to KDKC for management fees as of December 31, 2024, 2023 and 2022 was $69,472, $73,718 and $86,543 respectively. The Company is thus dependent on KDKC and its common ownership for all business functions. KDKC also fulfills website purchases arising from former franchise locations. The Company periodically makes cash advances to KDKC. Such cash advances are unsecured, non-interest bearing, and have no specific repayment terms. No advances were outstanding for the years ended December 31, 2024, 2023 and 2022.

key_facts.advance_terms: Unsecured, non-interest bearing, no specific repayment terms
key_facts.operational_dependency: All business and day-to-day operations performed by KDKC employees

notes.title: Concentrations of Credit Risk
notes.detail: The Company may be subject to credit risk related to its cash and cash equivalents, which are placed with high credit-quality financial institutions. From time to time, the Company may have amounts on deposit in excess of the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC) limit of $250,000. Management believes the Company is not exposed to any significant credit risk on cash and cash equivalents, nor has it experienced any losses.

notes.title: Intangible Assets
notes.detail: The Company purchased a franchise from its previous owner in December 2023. The reacquired franchise rights are amortized over a useful life of 15 years and are classified as a noncurrent asset in the accompanying balance sheet.

notes.title: Contingencies
notes.detail: In the normal course of business, the Company may be involved in legal proceedings. In the opinion of management, any liability from such proceedings would not have a material adverse effect on the Company's financial statements.

accounting_policies_summary.revenue_recognition: Franchise fees recognized over 5-year term (70% year 1, 30% years 2-5). Royalties at time of underlying sales. Renewals when received. Transfers 100% at time of transfer.
accounting_policies_summary.depreciation_amortization: Reacquired franchise rights amortized straight-line over 15 years ($1,667/year). No other depreciable assets.
accounting_policies_summary.impairment: Not specifically discussed — no tangible long-lived assets beyond intangible.
accounting_policies_summary.income_tax: S-corporation — no provision. Tax returns open for 3 years.
accounting_policies_summary.lease_accounting: No material ROU assets or liabilities as of December 31, 2024.
accounting_policies_summary.advertising_fund: Not applicable — no advertising fund exists.
accounting_policies_summary.credit_losses: ASU 2016-13 adopted January 1, 2023. No material impact. No allowance for doubtful accounts recorded.
